market by itself, for example, wa
rule_text: A trader should incorporate analysis of related financial markets (dollar, bonds, commodities) as leading indicators or confirmation signals before making directional decisions in a primary market (stocks), rather than analyzing that market in isolation.
entry_conditions: Stock chart shows primary trend signal (e.g., breakout, MA cross)
Dollar index aligns with stock signal direction
Bond yields/prices align with stock signal direction
Commodity index aligns with stock signal direction
exit_conditions: Any of the three intermarket relationships breaks alignment
Nonconfirmation appears in 2+ related markets

rule_text: A trader should incorporate analysis of related financial markets (dollar, bonds, commodities) as leading indicators or confirmation signals before making directional decisions in a primary market (stocks), rather than analyzing that market in isolation.
entry_conditions: Stock chart shows primary trend signal
At least 2 of 3 related markets (dollar, bonds, commodities) align with stock signal
No market shows explicit nonconfirmation
exit_conditions: Two or more intermarket relationships show nonconfirmation
Primary trend reversal occurs in any related market

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
Some understanding of how the different asset classes interact with each other is
important for at least two reasons. First, such an understanding helps you appreciate
how other �nancial markets in�ue
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket relationships—specifically bond-stock correlation inversions—because turns in the bond market precede turns in the stock market, and falling bond yields (rising bond prices) signal negative risk to equity positions.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines for 5+ consecutive trading days
Bond price appreciation exceeds 1.5% over the period
Stock index remains in uptrend (higher highs, higher lows)
exit_conditions: Exit long equity positions at market open following 5-day confirmation
Or exit 50% at signal, 50% on first lower high in stocks

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
Some understanding of how the different asset classes interact with each other is
important for at least two reasons. First, such an understanding helps you appreciate
how other �nancial markets in�ue
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket relationships—specifically bond-stock correlation inversions—because turns in the bond market precede turns in the stock market, and falling bond yields (rising bond prices) signal negative risk to equity positions.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines for 2-3 consecutive trading days
Bond price appreciation exceeds 0.8% over the period
Stock index shows weakness (lower close, elevated selling volume relative to 20-day avg)
exit_conditions: Reduce equity position by 33% on signal
Exit remaining 67% if yields continue lower or stock index closes below 5-day moving average

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:

important for at least two reasons. First, such an understanding helps you appreciate
how other �nancial markets in�ue
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket relationships—specifically bond-stock correlation inversions—because turns in the bond market precede turns in the stock market, and falling bond yields (rising bond prices) signal negative risk to equity positions.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines for 1 trading day
Bond price appreciation exceeds 0.3% intraday
Stock index shows any close below prior close (intraday confirmation not required)
exit_conditions: Flatten entire equity position immediately or at next signal bar open
Re-enter only after bonds stabilize (2-day yield flat or rising)

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
Figure 1.1 compares the yield on the 10‐year Treasury note to the S&P 500 during
2000. After peaking that January (�rst arrow), the bond yield started falling a lot
faster than the stock market. By th
rule_text: Bond yields are a leading indicator of equity market direction at major turning points; falling bond yields precede stock market declines and provide early warning signals.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines 50+ basis points over 4-8 weeks
S&P 500 remains flat or in sideways consolidation during same period
Yield decline rate exceeds equity decline rate by 2:1 or greater
Confirm with second intermarket indicator (e.g., dollar strength, credit spreads)
exit_conditions: Bond yields stabilize or bounce higher for 2+ weeks
Equity market breaks out above consolidation range

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
Figure 1.1 compares the yield on the 10‐year Treasury note to the S&P 500 during
2000. After peaking that January (�rst arrow), the bond yield started falling a lot
faster than the stock market. By th
rule_text: Bond yields are a leading indicator of equity market direction at major turning points; falling bond yields precede stock market declines and provide early warning signals.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines 30+ basis points over 4-6 weeks
S&P 500 shows no corresponding decline (flat to +2% during same period)
Yield decline rate exceeds equity decline rate by 1.5:1 or greater
Optional confirmation: treasury outperformance vs equities on relative strength
exit_conditions: Bond yields stabilize for 1+ week
Equity market rallies above pre-divergence level

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30

2000. After peaking that January (�rst arrow), the bond yield started falling a lot
faster than the stock market. By th
rule_text: Bond yields are a leading indicator of equity market direction at major turning points; falling bond yields precede stock market declines and provide early warning signals.
entry_conditions: 10-year Treasury yield declines 20+ basis points over 2-4 weeks
S&P 500 remains unchanged or positive during same period
Yield decline velocity (bps/week) exceeds prior 12-week average by 1.5x
No confirmation required; enter on divergence signal alone
exit_conditions: Stop loss: yields bounce +15 bps from entry signal
Profit taking: reduce at 50% if equities decline 3-5%

rule_text: Monitor leading intermarket indicators (bonds, commodities, currency) to anticipate directional changes in your primary market before they occur.
entry_conditions: Bond yield trend reverses for 2+ consecutive weekly closes
Commodity index diverges from stock index for 3+ weeks
USD index shows sustained directional move (2+ weeks)
At least 2 of 3 conditions must align
exit_conditions: Primary market (stocks) confirms the intermarket signal with its own directional move (2+ weeks in predicted direction)
Or intermarket signal reverses

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
changes direction before stocks at major turning points and is often a leading
indicator of the stock market. Figure 1.1 also demonstrates why it’s so important for
stock analysts to take trends in th
rule_text: Monitor leading intermarket indicators (bonds, commodities, currency) to anticipate directional changes in your primary market before they occur.
entry_conditions: Bond yields decline while stocks rally (or vice versa) for 5+ consecutive days
OR commodity prices spike >3% while stock trends flat/declining
OR USD strengthens while foreign equity index outperforms domestic stocks
exit_conditions: Primary market begins to move in direction predicted by intermarket signal (3-5 days confirmation)
Or original intermarket signal reverses on close

changes direction before stocks at major turning points and is often a leading
indicator of the stock market. Figure 1.1 also demonstrates why it’s so important for
stock analysts to take trends in th
rule_text: Monitor leading intermarket indicators (bonds, commodities, currency) to anticipate directional changes in your primary market before they occur.
entry_conditions: Bond yields reverse intraday while stocks continue in previous direction
OR commodity index gaps up/down >2% while stocks show weakness/strength divergence
OR USD makes single-day reversal after multi-day move
exit_conditions: Stock market fails to follow predicted direction within 1-2 trading days
Or take profit when stock trend reverses (first 2-3% move in predicted direction)

had a strong run since 2002.
Consider the relative performance of those four asset classes since the start of
2002 when the U.S. dollar started a major decline that eventually took it to a record

rule_text: A trader should establish long commodity positions when the U.S. dollar enters a downtrend, and conversely, reduce or exit commodity longs when the dollar trends upward, because commodities and the U.S. dollar trend in opposite directions.
entry_conditions: U.S. Dollar Index closes below its 200-day moving average
Dollar Index lower low is lower than prior lower low (confirmed downtrend)
CRB Commodity Index closes above its 50-day moving average
exit_conditions: U.S. Dollar Index closes above its 200-day moving average
CRB Commodity Index closes below its 50-day moving average

exit_conditions: U.S. Dollar Index closes above its 100-day moving average
CRB Commodity Index closes below its 30-day moving average

exit_conditions: U.S. Dollar Index closes above its 50-day moving average
CRB Commodity Index closes below its 14-day moving average

Figure 1.2 compares the trend of the U.S. Dollar Index to the CRB Index of
commodity prices between 2000 and 2008. It’s clear that the two markets trended
in opposite directions. It can also be seen t
rule_text: A trader should initiate long positions in commodities and commodity-linked currencies when the U.S. Dollar Index begins a sustained decline, as the inverse relationship between dollar strength and commodity prices is one of the most consistent intermarket relationships.
entry_conditions: Dollar Index closes below 50-period moving average on daily timeframe
Dollar Index makes a lower low relative to prior swing high (structural confirmation)
Commodity index (CRB) closes above its 50-period moving average
Both conditions confirmed on weekly timeframe as well
exit_conditions: Dollar Index closes above 50-period MA on daily
Commodity index closes below 50-period MA on daily
Trailing stop at 2% below entry

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch.1, p.24-30

commodity prices between 2000 and 2008. It’s clear that the two markets trended
in opposite directions. It can also be seen t
rule_text: A trader should initiate long positions in commodities and commodity-linked currencies when the U.S. Dollar Index begins a sustained decline, as the inverse relationship between dollar strength and commodity prices is one of the most consistent intermarket relationships.
entry_conditions: Dollar Index closes below 30-period moving average
Commodity index closes above 30-period moving average
Dollar Index has declined more than 2% from recent swing high
Sustained for minimum 3 consecutive daily closes
exit_conditions: Dollar Index closes back above 30-period MA
Stop loss at 3% below entry
Take profit at 5% above entry

rule_text: A trader should initiate long positions in commodities and commodity-linked currencies when the U.S. Dollar Index begins a sustained decline, as the inverse relationship between dollar strength and commodity prices is one of the most consistent intermarket relationships.
entry_conditions: Dollar Index closes below 20-period moving average
Commodity index shows any upturn (closes above prior day)
Dollar Index down 1% or more from recent swing high
Single bar confirmation
exit_conditions: Stop loss at 2% below entry
Take profit target at 3% above entry
Dollar Index retest of recent highs

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, p. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
the leaders and out of the laggards. You’ll also learn how tracking sector rotation
offers valuable insights into the direction of the stock market and the economy.
Near the start of a new bull market
rule_text: A trader should monitor sector rotation leadership patterns as a market regime indicator, because sector leadership sequence (cyclicals leading at bottoms, energy leading near tops) reveals inflection points in bull/bear market cycles.
entry_conditions: Energy sector 4-week relative strength > 60th percentile vs broad market
Energy sector outperformance confirmed over last 3 consecutive weeks
Cyclical stocks (consumer discretionary, technology, transportation) showing relative weakness (< 40th percentile)
Bull market in place for minimum 12+ months (established trend)
exit_conditions: Energy leadership reverts to historical mean (50th percentile) for 2 consecutive weeks
Cyclicals resume outperformance for 2 weeks

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, p. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
the leaders and out of the laggards. You’ll also learn how tracking sector rotation
offers valuable insights into the direction of the stock market and the economy.
Near the start of a new bull market
rule_text: A trader should monitor sector rotation leadership patterns as a market regime indicator, because sector leadership sequence (cyclicals leading at bottoms, energy leading near tops) reveals inflection points in bull/bear market cycles.
entry_conditions: Energy sector 2-week relative strength > 55th percentile vs S&P 500
Consumer discretionary + technology + transportation combined relative strength < 45th percentile
Bull market duration 6+ months (mid-to-late cycle)
Energy outperformance confirmed for 2 consecutive weeks
exit_conditions: Energy relative strength drops below 50th percentile for 1 week
Early-cycle leaders (tech, consumer discretionary) resume outperformance

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, p. 24-30

the leaders and out of the laggards. You’ll also learn how tracking sector rotation
offers valuable insights into the direction of the stock market and the economy.

rule_text: A trader should monitor sector rotation leadership patterns as a market regime indicator, because sector leadership sequence (cyclicals leading at bottoms, energy leading near tops) reveals inflection points in bull/bear market cycles.
entry_conditions: Energy sector 1-week relative strength > 52nd percentile vs S&P 500
Prior 4 weeks show cyclical leadership fading (tech/consumer disc < 48th percentile)
Any bull market environment (minimum 3 months duration)
Energy outperformance evident in most recent week
exit_conditions: Energy leadership does not persist to week 2 (reverts below 52nd percentile)
Cyclicals resume strength immediately

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch.1, p.24-30
raw_chunk_text:
in stocks. Energy leadership is almost always a dangerous warning sign for the stock
market. One of the ways to tell that the stock market is peaking is when money
starts to �ow out of energy stocks a
rule_text: A trader should recognize sector rotation from growth to defensive sectors as an early warning signal that the stock market is approaching a peak, and should act on this intermarket shift before the broader market decline.
entry_conditions: Energy sector underperforms broad market by 3%+ over trailing 20 days
Consumer staples, health care, and utilities sectors outperform energy by 5%+ cumulatively
Relative strength of defensive basket (XLP+XLV+XLU)/XLE > 1.05
Confirmed over minimum 15 trading days of observation
exit_conditions: Rotation reverses (energy outperforms by 2%+ for 5 consecutive days)
Market makes new all-time high after rotation signal
Time-based exit after 30 calendar days

rule_text: A trader should recognize sector rotation from growth to defensive sectors as an early warning signal that the stock market is approaching a peak, and should act on this intermarket shift before the broader market decline.

Consumer staples, health care, and utilities sectors outperform energy by 3%+ cumulatively
Relative strength of defensive basket (XLP+XLV+XLU)/XLE > 1.03
Confirmed over minimum 10 trading days of observation
exit_conditions: Rotation reverses (energy outperforms by 1%+ for 3 consecutive days)
Market makes new all-time high after rotation signal
Time-based exit after 20 calendar days

rule_text: A trader should recognize sector rotation from growth to defensive sectors as an early warning signal that the stock market is approaching a peak, and should act on this intermarket shift before the broader market decline.

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
The same is true at market bottoms. The ending of the last two recessions during
2003 and 2009 followed market upturns a few months earlier. When the stock
market weakens, money tends to rotate out of
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket money rotation patterns (stocks↔bonds, bonds↔commodities) as leading indicators of market regime transitions, because asset class peaks and troughs follow a predictable cyclical order during business cycle turns.
entry_conditions: Bond yield trend reverses from down to up (or vice versa) over 20+ trading days
Stock/Bond relative strength ratio crosses its 50-day moving average
Commodity index shows opposite directional pressure to stocks for 15+ consecutive days
exit_conditions: Rotation signal reverses back to prior regime for 10+ consecutive days
Stop loss at -2% from entry

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
The same is true at market bottoms. The ending of the last two recessions during
2003 and 2009 followed market upturns a few months earlier. When the stock
market weakens, money tends to rotate out of
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket money rotation patterns (stocks↔bonds, bonds↔commodities) as leading indicators of market regime transitions, because asset class peaks and troughs follow a predictable cyclical order during business cycle turns.
entry_conditions: Bond/stock relative strength ratio (TLT/SPY or equivalent) crosses its 20-day moving average
Intramarket: yield curve steepness changes by 15+ bps over 5 trading days
Volume in contrarian asset class (bonds during stock weakness) exceeds 30-day average by 20%+
exit_conditions: Rotation reverses for 5+ consecutive days
Stop loss at -1.5% from entry

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch. 1, pp. 24-30
raw_chunk_text:
The same is true at market bottoms. The ending of the last two recessions during
2003 and 2009 followed market upturns a few months earlier. When the stock
market weakens, money tends to rotate out of
rule_text: A trader should monitor intermarket money rotation patterns (stocks↔bonds, bonds↔commodities) as leading indicators of market regime transitions, because asset class peaks and troughs follow a predictable cyclical order during business cycle turns.
entry_conditions: Bond/stock relative strength ratio crosses its 5-day moving average
Daily change in TLT/SPY spread exceeds 1.5 standard deviations from 20-day mean
Single day: contrarian asset class shows 2x+ average volume
exit_conditions: Rotation reverses for 2+ consecutive days
Stop loss at -1.0% from entry
Time stop: exit after 15 calendar days regardless

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch.1, pp.24-30
raw_chunk_text:
JOHN’S TIPS
Bonds usually change direction �rst at tops and bottoms, stocks turn second, and
commodities third.
That knowledge will help you determine where to be at different stages of the business
c
rule_text: Monitor bond price direction changes as a leading indicator of business cycle shifts, then use subsequent stock and commodity directional confirmations to stage portfolio positioning across asset classes in sequence.
entry_conditions: Bond prices decline for 5+ consecutive trading days after an established uptrend
Stock index closes below its 20-day moving average (confirming bonds' lead)
Oil/commodity index breaks below its 20-day moving average (confirming sequence)
exit_conditions: Any asset class reverses back above its moving average, invalidating the sequence
Portfolio reaches target defensive allocation (50% equities, 30% bonds, 20% cash)

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch.1, pp.24-30
raw_chunk_text:
JOHN’S TIPS
Bonds usually change direction �rst at tops and bottoms, stocks turn second, and
commodities third.
That knowledge will help you determine where to be at different stages of the business
c
rule_text: Monitor bond price direction changes as a leading indicator of business cycle shifts, then use subsequent stock and commodity directional confirmations to stage portfolio positioning across asset classes in sequence.
entry_conditions: Bond index closes below its 10-day high and below 10-day moving average
Stock index breaks below 10-day moving average within 10 days of bond signal
Oil closes below 10-day moving average within 15 days of stock signal
exit_conditions: 10-day moving average crossed back upward by price
Target defensive allocation reached (40% equities, 40% bonds, 20% cash)

source_citation: Intermarket Analysis, John Murphy, Ch.1, pp.24-30
raw_chunk_text:
JOHN’S TIPS
Bonds usually change direction �rst at tops and bottoms, stocks turn second, and
commodities third.
That knowledge will help you determine where to be at different stages of the business
c
rule_text: Monitor bond price direction changes as a leading indicator of business cycle shifts, then use subsequent stock and commodity directional confirmations to stage portfolio positioning across asset classes in sequence.
entry_conditions: Bond index closes below 5-day high (early peak detection)
Stock index closes below 5-day moving average within 3 days of bond signal (minimal lag confirmation)
Oil directional bias can confirm but is not required for position entry
exit_conditions: 5-day moving average crossed back upward
Stop loss at 2% portfolio drawdown from entry
